question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the URL construction logic in buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() where strings.TrimRight(base, '/') and strings.HasSuffix(base, '/api/v1') are used, by injecting a mirrorRest URL with a crafted path suffix that bypasses the suffix check and appends '/network/nodes' to an attacker-controlled endpoint?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST to an HTTP URL (not HTTPS) pointing to an attacker-controlled server that returns a crafted nodesEnvelope JSON, causing hiero.ClientForNetwork(netmap) to connect to attacker nodes and expose signed transfer transactions that trigger staking reward payouts?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of response body size limits in json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&payload) to serve an extremely large nodesEnvelope JSON response that causes memory exhaustion in the pinger process, preventing it from establishing a client and blocking all staking reward collection?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the nodesEnvelope.Links.Next field is parsed but never followed (pagination is ignored), causing the pinger to only see a partial node list returned by an attacker-controlled mirror REST endpoint, where all legitimate nodes are omitted and only attacker-controlled nodes appear in the first page?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that hiero.ClientForName(cfg.network) for 'mainnet'/'testnet'/'previewnet' relies entirely on the hiero-sdk-go library's built-in node list without any runtime verification, so that a supply-chain compromise of the github.com/hiero-ledger/hiero-sdk-go/v2/sdk dependency injects attacker-controlled nodes that intercept staking-reward-triggering transactions?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ticker-based transfer loop in main.go where submitWithRetry() is called every cfg.interval, by setting HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_INTERVAL to a very short duration (e.g., 1ms) to flood the network with transfer transactions, causing the operator account to pay excessive fees that deplete the balance needed to collect staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any check on the sign of cfg.amountTinybar (only zero is rejected in loadConfig()) to set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_AMOUNT_TINYBAR to a negative value, causing AddHbarTransfer(toID, hiero.HbarFromTinybar(cfg.amountTinybar)) to credit the operator and debit the destination, reversing the intended transfer direction and potentially manipulating staking reward accounting?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the operatorKey default value is a well-known hardcoded key in config.go (loaded via envOr()), meaning any deployment that fails to override HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_OPERATOR_KEY uses a publicly known private key, allowing the attacker to sign transactions as the operator and claim all accumulated staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the http.Client{Timeout: cfg.mirrorNodeClientTimeout} configuration by serving a slow HTTP response that times out, triggering the retry loop in buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes(), and on a subsequent retry serving a malicious node list that gets accepted, causing staking-reward-triggering transfers to be routed to attacker nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the retry condition 'retry := resp.StatusCode == http.StatusTooManyRequests || resp.StatusCode >= 500' to serve a 429 response followed by a malicious 200 response with a crafted node list, causing the pinger to accept attacker-controlled nodes after the retry?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the backoff in buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() uses 'cfg.mirrorNodeClientBaseBackoff * time.Duration(1<<(attempt-1))' without a cap (unlike the transfer backoff which caps at 30s), causing unbounded delay in node topology resolution and blocking staking reward collection indefinitely?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the config struct stores operatorKey as a plain string field with no memory protection, allowing an attacker with local access (e.g., via a container escape or shared process namespace) to read the key from process memory and use it to claim the operator's unpaid staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the flag.Parse() call in loadConfig() which reads command-line arguments, by injecting -operator-key or -operator-id flags through a process invocation they control (e.g., in a CI/CD pipeline or container entrypoint), overriding the operator identity and redirecting staking reward collection?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that hiero.AccountIDFromString(cfg.toAccountID) in submitWithRetry() accepts any valid Hedera account ID format without verifying it is the intended staking reward account, allowing an attacker who controls the environment to set toAccountID to their own account and receive both the transferred HBAR and the triggered staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the retry loop 'for i := 1; i <= attempts; i++' in submitWithRetry() where each failed attempt re-executes the same transfer with the same toID, meaning if an attacker has temporarily redirected the destination account, all retry attempts also send funds to the attacker's account?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any network integrity check after hiero.ClientForNetwork(netmap) is called, meaning the client never re-validates the node list during its lifetime, so a one-time MITM attack on the initial buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() call permanently compromises all subsequent staking-reward-triggering transfers?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 'if len(network) == 0 { return nil, false, fmt.Errorf(...) }' check by serving a nodesEnvelope where all entries have ep.Port == 0 or empty hosts (causing them to be skipped), resulting in an empty network map error that prevents the pinger from starting and blocks all staking reward collection?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that hiero.PrivateKeyFromString() accepts multiple key formats (ED25519, ECDSA, DER-encoded) without the caller specifying the expected format, allowing an attacker to inject a key string in an unexpected format that the SDK parses as a different key than intended, binding the client to an attacker-controlled signing key?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the transfer transaction built by hiero.NewTransferTransaction().AddHbarTransfer().AddHbarTransfer() never sets a memo or transaction ID prefix that would allow the operator to distinguish legitimate pinger transfers from attacker-replayed transactions, enabling replay attacks that trigger staking rewards at unintended times?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the default operatorID '0.0.2' in config.go, which is a well-known genesis account on Hedera, combined with the hardcoded default operatorKey, to impersonate the genesis account and claim any staking rewards accumulated by that account?
